questTitle: Sec 4 Chemistry: Rate of Chemical Reaction
subject: Chemistry
description: Master the concepts of rate of chemical reaction! This worksheet covers factors affecting reaction rate, collision theory explanations, activation energy, and interpretation of rate graphs. Read each question carefully and apply your understanding of particle theory.

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Understanding Rate of Reaction
questions.question: What is the definition of rate of reaction?
options: The total amount of product formed in a reaction
The change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time
The total time taken for a reaction to complete
The energy required to start a reaction
questions.answer: The change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Understanding Rate of Reaction
questions.question: Which of the following units can be used to express rate of reaction?
options: g/min or cm³/s
g or cm³
mol or dm³
J/mol or kJ
questions.answer: g/min or cm³/s

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Understanding Rate of Reaction
questions.question: According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must:
options: Be heated to 100°C
Collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation
Be in the same physical state
Have the same mass
questions.answer: Collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Understanding Rate of Reaction
questions.question: A collision that results in a chemical reaction is called:
options: An elastic collision
An ineffective collision
An effective or successful collision
A physical collision
questions.answer: An effective or successful collision

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Understanding Rate of Reaction - Test Question
questions.question: In a reaction between marble chips and hydrochloric acid, 50 cm³ of gas was collected in 25 seconds. What is the average rate of reaction?
options: 0.5 cm³/s
2 cm³/s
25 cm³/s
1250 cm³/s
questions.answer: 2 cm³/s

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Activation Energy
questions.question: What is activation energy (Eₐ)?
options: The energy released when bonds are formed
The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur
The total energy of the products
The energy stored in the reactants
questions.answer: The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Activation Energy
questions.question: On an energy profile diagram, where is the activation energy represented?
options: The difference between reactant energy and product energy
The difference between the peak (transition state) and reactant energy
The total height of the diagram
The difference between the peak and product energy
questions.answer: The difference between the peak (transition state) and reactant energy

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Activation Energy
questions.question: If a reaction has a high activation energy, what does this mean?
options: The reaction will occur very quickly
Few particles will have enough energy to react, so the reaction is slow
The reaction releases a lot of heat
The reaction does not require a catalyst
questions.answer: Few particles will have enough energy to react, so the reaction is slow

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Activation Energy
questions.question: For an effective collision to occur, the energy of the colliding particles must be:
options: Less than the activation energy
Equal to or greater than the activation energy
Equal to zero
Equal to the energy of the products
questions.answer: Equal to or greater than the activation energy

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Activation Energy - Test Question
questions.question: Two reactions X and Y have activation energies of 50 kJ/mol and 120 kJ/mol respectively at the same temperature. Which statement is correct?
options: Reaction Y is faster because it has higher activation energy
Reaction X is faster because more particles have energy ≥ 50 kJ/mol than ≥ 120 kJ/mol
Both reactions proceed at the same rate
The rate cannot be compared without knowing the concentration
questions.answer: Reaction X is faster because more particles have energy ≥ 50 kJ/mol than ≥ 120 kJ/mol

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Effect of Concentration
questions.question: When the concentration of a reactant is increased, what happens to the rate of reaction?
options: Rate decreases
Rate increases
Rate stays the same
Rate first increases then decreases
questions.answer: Rate increases

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Effect of Concentration
questions.question: Using collision theory, explain why increasing concentration increases the rate of reaction.
options: Particles move faster and collide with more energy
There are more particles per unit volume, leading to more frequent collisions
The activation energy is lowered
The particles become smaller and collide more easily
questions.answer: There are more particles per unit volume, leading to more frequent collisions

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Effect of Concentration
questions.question: Magnesium ribbon is added to dilute hydrochloric acid. What would be observed if the concentration of acid is doubled?
options: Slower fizzing and less heat produced
Faster fizzing and magnesium disappears more quickly
No change in the rate of fizzing
The reaction stops completely
questions.answer: Faster fizzing and magnesium disappears more quickly

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Effect of Concentration
questions.question: For gases, increasing pressure has a similar effect to increasing concentration. Why?
options: Higher pressure increases the temperature
Higher pressure forces particles closer together, increasing the number of particles per unit volume
Higher pressure lowers the activation energy
Higher pressure makes particles lighter
questions.answer: Higher pressure forces particles closer together, increasing the number of particles per unit volume

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Effect of Concentration - Test Question
questions.question: In an experiment, marble chips react with 1 mol/dm³ HCl and 2 mol/dm³ HCl separately. Both experiments use excess marble chips and the same volume of acid. Which statement is correct about the final volume of CO₂ collected?
options: 2 mol/dm³ HCl produces more CO₂ because the rate is faster
1 mol/dm³ HCl produces more CO₂ because the reaction is slower
Both produce the same final volume of CO₂, but 2 mol/dm³ HCl reaches it faster
Both produce the same final volume of CO₂ at the same time
questions.answer: Both produce the same final volume of CO₂, but 2 mol/dm³ HCl reaches it faster

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Effect of Temperature
questions.question: When the temperature of a reaction mixture is increased, what happens to the rate of reaction?
options: Rate decreases
Rate increases
Rate stays the same
Rate becomes zero
questions.answer: Rate increases

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Effect of Temperature
questions.question: Using collision theory, which is the MAIN reason why increasing temperature increases the rate of reaction?
options: Particles become larger
The activation energy is lowered
Particles move faster, collide more frequently, and more particles have energy ≥ Eₐ
The concentration of particles increases
questions.answer: Particles move faster, collide more frequently, and more particles have energy ≥ Eₐ

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Effect of Temperature
questions.question: At a higher temperature, the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve shifts. What happens to the proportion of particles with energy greater than or equal to the activation energy?
options: It decreases
It increases
It stays the same
It becomes zero
questions.answer: It increases

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Effect of Temperature
questions.question: A general rule states that for every 10°C rise in temperature, the rate of reaction approximately:
options: Stays the same
Doubles
Halves
Triples
questions.answer: Doubles

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Effect of Temperature - Test Question
questions.question: Experiment A is conducted at 25°C and Experiment B at 45°C. Both use the same reactants and concentrations. Using the general rule about temperature and rate, approximately how many times faster is Experiment B compared to A?
options: 2 times faster
4 times faster
8 times faster
20 times faster
questions.answer: 4 times faster

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Effect of Surface Area (Particle Size)
questions.question: Marble chips are available as large lumps or powder. Which form reacts faster with hydrochloric acid?
options: Large lumps
Powder
Both react at the same rate
Neither reacts with acid
questions.answer: Powder

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Effect of Surface Area (Particle Size)
questions.question: Using collision theory, explain why smaller particles (larger surface area) react faster.
options: Smaller particles have higher energy
More surface is exposed for reactant particles to collide with, increasing collision frequency
Smaller particles lower the activation energy
Smaller particles increase the concentration
questions.answer: More surface is exposed for reactant particles to collide with, increasing collision frequency

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Effect of Surface Area (Particle Size)
questions.question: A cube of side 2 cm has a surface area of 24 cm². If it is cut into 8 smaller cubes of side 1 cm each, what is the total surface area now?
options: 24 cm²
36 cm²
48 cm²
96 cm²
questions.answer: 48 cm²

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Effect of Surface Area (Particle Size)
questions.question: In industry, why is it dangerous to have fine powders of combustible materials (like flour or coal dust) in the air?
options: They react slowly and clog machinery
The large surface area can cause rapid combustion or explosions
They lower the temperature of the surroundings
They reduce the oxygen concentration
questions.answer: The large surface area can cause rapid combustion or explosions

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Effect of Surface Area (Particle Size) - Test Question
questions.question: 10 g of marble chips (large lumps) and 10 g of marble powder react separately with excess dilute HCl. Which statement correctly compares the two reactions?
options: Powder reacts faster and produces more CO₂
Lumps react faster but produce less CO₂
Both produce the same total volume of CO₂, but powder reaches the final volume faster
Lumps produce more CO₂ because they have more mass
questions.answer: Both produce the same total volume of CO₂, but powder reaches the final volume faster

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Effect of Catalyst
questions.question: What is a catalyst?
options: A substance that slows down a reaction
A substance that speeds up a reaction without being chemically changed at the end
A substance that is used up during the reaction
A substance that increases the yield of products
questions.answer: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being chemically changed at the end

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Effect of Catalyst
questions.question: How does a catalyst increase the rate of reaction?
options: By increasing the temperature of the reaction
By providing an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy
By increasing the concentration of reactants
By increasing the surface area of reactants
questions.answer: By providing an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Effect of Catalyst
questions.question: On a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve, adding a catalyst causes:
options: The curve to shift to the right
The curve to become taller
The activation energy line to move to the left (lower Eₐ), so more particles have E ≥ Eₐ
The curve to shift to the left
questions.answer: The activation energy line to move to the left (lower Eₐ), so more particles have E ≥ Eₐ

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Effect of Catalyst
questions.question: Manganese dioxide (MnO₂) is used as a catalyst in the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide. After the reaction, what happens to the MnO₂?
options: It is used up and cannot be recovered
It is chemically unchanged and can be reused
It is converted into a different compound
It dissolves in the products
questions.answer: It is chemically unchanged and can be reused

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Effect of Catalyst - Test Question
questions.question: Hydrogen peroxide decomposes with and without a catalyst. Which statement correctly describes the effect of the catalyst on the energy profile diagram?
options: The catalyst lowers both the activation energy and the enthalpy change
The catalyst lowers the activation energy but does not change the enthalpy change
The catalyst increases the activation energy and increases the enthalpy change
The catalyst does not affect the activation energy but increases the rate
questions.answer: The catalyst lowers the activation energy but does not change the enthalpy change

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Volume/Mass vs Time
questions.question: In a graph of volume of gas produced against time, what does a steeper curve indicate?
options: Slower rate of reaction
Faster rate of reaction
No reaction occurring
The reaction is reversible
questions.answer: Faster rate of reaction

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Volume/Mass vs Time
questions.question: In a graph of volume of gas against time, the curve eventually becomes horizontal (flat). What does this indicate?
options: The reaction is speeding up
The reaction has stopped because one reactant is used up
The temperature has increased
A catalyst has been added
questions.answer: The reaction has stopped because one reactant is used up

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Volume/Mass vs Time
questions.question: Two experiments produce the same final volume of gas, but Experiment A has a steeper initial curve than Experiment B. What can be concluded?
options: Experiment A used more reactant
Experiment A had a faster initial rate but the same total amount of product
Experiment B had a faster rate
Experiment A used a catalyst that increased the yield
questions.answer: Experiment A had a faster initial rate but the same total amount of product

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Volume/Mass vs Time
questions.question: In a graph of mass of reaction mixture against time (measuring mass loss), the mass decreases over time. Why does the curve eventually become horizontal?
options: Gas is no longer escaping
The reaction has completed and no more gas is being produced
The balance is broken
The temperature has dropped
questions.answer: The reaction has completed and no more gas is being produced

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Volume/Mass vs Time - Test Question
questions.question: The graph shows volume of CO₂ against time for two experiments. Curve P reaches 60 cm³ in 30 s then levels off. Curve Q reaches 60 cm³ in 60 s then levels off. Which statement is correct?
options: P used higher concentration of acid than Q
Q used higher concentration of acid than P
P used less marble chips than Q
Q had a higher temperature than P
questions.answer: P used higher concentration of acid than Q

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Comparing Conditions
questions.question: Two experiments use the same mass of marble chips and volume of acid. Experiment 1 uses large lumps, Experiment 2 uses powder. On a volume vs time graph, how would the two curves compare?
options: Both curves are identical
Experiment 2 curve is steeper initially but both reach the same final volume
Experiment 1 curve is steeper and reaches a higher final volume
Experiment 2 reaches a higher final volume
questions.answer: Experiment 2 curve is steeper initially but both reach the same final volume

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Comparing Conditions
questions.question: Experiment X is conducted at 30°C and Experiment Y at 50°C. Both use identical reactants and quantities. On a volume vs time graph, how would the curves compare?
options: Both curves are identical
Curve Y is steeper initially but both reach the same final volume
Curve X reaches a higher final volume
Curve Y reaches a lower final volume
questions.answer: Curve Y is steeper initially but both reach the same final volume

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Comparing Conditions
questions.question: Experiment A uses 50 cm³ of 1 mol/dm³ HCl with excess marble chips. Experiment B uses 50 cm³ of 2 mol/dm³ HCl with excess marble chips. How do the final volumes of CO₂ compare?
options: Both produce the same final volume
Experiment B produces double the final volume
Experiment A produces double the final volume
Final volume cannot be determined
questions.answer: Experiment B produces double the final volume

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Comparing Conditions
questions.question: A catalyst is added to a reaction. On a volume vs time graph, how does the curve with catalyst compare to the curve without catalyst?
options: The curve with catalyst is steeper and reaches a higher final volume
The curve with catalyst is steeper but reaches the same final volume
The curve with catalyst is less steep but reaches a higher final volume
Both curves are identical
questions.answer: The curve with catalyst is steeper but reaches the same final volume

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Interpreting Rate Graphs - Comparing Conditions - Test Question
questions.question: The graph below shows three curves P, Q, and R for the reaction of marble chips with hydrochloric acid. All use the same mass of marble chips. Curve P: steepest, levels off at 80 cm³. Curve Q: moderate steepness, levels off at 80 cm³. Curve R: least steep, levels off at 40 cm³. Which statement correctly identifies the conditions?
options: P: high temperature, high concentration. Q: low temperature, high concentration. R: low temperature, low concentration.
P: high concentration, large surface area. Q: low concentration, large surface area. R: same concentration as Q, but half the volume of acid.
P: with catalyst. Q: without catalyst. R: without catalyst but lower temperature.
P: powder form. Q: small lumps. R: large lumps with double the acid concentration.
questions.answer: P: high concentration, large surface area. Q: low concentration, large surface area. R: same concentration as Q, but half the volume of acid.
